findings.description: No clause was found identifying the subject-matter of the processing.
findings.explanation: Article 28(3) requires the controller-processor contract to set out the subject-matter and duration of the processing, the nature and purpose, the type of personal data, the categories of data subjects, and the obligations and rights of the controller.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'Subject-matter of Processing' clause or an Annex describing the services giving rise to processing.
findings.title: Subject-matter of processing not identified

findings.description: No clause was found specifying how long the processing will continue.
findings.explanation: Article 28(3) requires the duration of the processing to be set out.
findings.recommendation: State that processing continues for the term of the agreement plus a defined wind-down period.
findings.title: Duration of processing not specified

findings.description: No clause was found describing the nature and purpose of the processing.
findings.explanation: Art. 28(3) requires the nature and purpose of the processing to be stated.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'Nature and Purpose of Processing' clause naming the services and processing operations.
findings.title: Nature and purpose of processing not described

findings.description: No clause was found stating the obligations and rights of the controller.
findings.explanation: Art. 28(3) requires the controller's obligations and rights to be set out.
findings.recommendation: Add a clause cross-referencing the controller's responsibilities under the agreement and GDPR.
findings.title: Obligations and rights of the controller not stated

findings.description: No clause was found requiring processing only on documented instructions from the controller.
findings.explanation: Art. 28(3)(a) requires the processor to act only on documented instructions, including for international transfers (subject to law).
findings.recommendation: Add: 'Processor shall process Personal Data only on documented instructions from Controller.'
findings.title: Documented-instructions clause missing

findings.description: No clause was found requiring authorised persons to be subject to confidentiality.
findings.explanation: Art. 28(3)(b) requires personnel processing personal data to be bound by confidentiality.
findings.recommendation: Add: 'Processor shall ensure that persons authorised to process Personal Data have committed themselves to confidentiality.'
findings.title: Confidentiality-commitment clause missing

findings.description: No clause was found requiring deletion or return of personal data at end of services.
findings.explanation: Art. 28(3)(g) requires the processor, at the choice of the controller, to delete or return all the personal data to the controller after the end of the provision of services.
findings.recommendation: Add a clause: 'At the choice of the Controller, Processor shall delete or return all Personal Data after the end of the provision of services relating to processing.'
findings.title: Deletion-or-return clause missing

findings.description: No clause was found requiring the processor to make information available demonstrating Art. 28 compliance.
findings.explanation: Art. 28(3)(h) requires the processor to make available to the controller all information necessary to demonstrate compliance with Article 28 and to allow for and contribute to audits, including inspections.
findings.recommendation: Add: 'Processor shall make available to Controller all information necessary to demonstrate compliance with Article 28 and shall allow for and contribute to audits, including inspections, conducted by Controller or its mandated auditor.'
findings.title: Compliance-demonstration clause missing

findings.description: No clause was found giving Controller notification of subprocessor changes and a right to object.
findings.explanation: Art. 28(2) requires informing the controller of intended subprocessor changes and giving an opportunity to object.
findings.recommendation: Add: 'Processor shall inform Controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors with reasonable notice, giving Controller the opportunity to object.'
findings.title: Subprocessor change notification / objection missing

findings.description: No clause was found imposing the same data-protection obligations on subprocessors.
findings.explanation: Art. 28(4) requires the processor to impose, by contract or other legal act, the same data-protection obligations on the subprocessor as set out in the contract between the controller and processor.
findings.recommendation: Add: 'Where Processor engages a Sub-processor, the same data protection obligations shall be imposed on the Sub-processor by contract.'
findings.title: Subprocessor flow-down clause missing

findings.description: No clause was found requiring the processor to notify the controller of a personal data breach.
findings.explanation: Art. 33(2) requires the processor to notify the controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a personal data breach.
findings.recommendation: Add: 'Processor shall notify Controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a Personal Data Breach.'
findings.title: Processor breach-notification clause missing

findings.description: No reference to SCC Clause 1 / Purpose and Scope was found.
findings.explanation: Decision 2021/914 requires Clause 1 (Purpose and Scope) — it sets out the parties' agreement to be bound by the SCCs.
findings.recommendation: Include the full Clause 1 (Purpose and Scope) text unmodified.
findings.title: SCC Clause 1 (Purpose and Scope) missing

findings.description: No reference to SCC Clause 2 / Effect and Invariability was found.
findings.explanation: Clause 2 forbids material modification — the parties may add other clauses or business-related terms only if they do not contradict the SCCs.
findings.recommendation: Include the full Clause 2 (Effect and Invariability) text unmodified.
findings.title: SCC Clause 2 (Effect and Invariability) missing

findings.description: No reference to SCC Clause 18 was found.
findings.explanation: Clause 18 sets the governing law (must be an EU Member State allowing third-party-beneficiary rights) and forum.
findings.recommendation: Include the full Clause 18 text and pick a qualifying EU Member State.
findings.title: SCC Clause 18 (Governing Law / Forum) missing

findings.description: No reference to a TIA / Transfer Risk Assessment was found.
findings.explanation: EDPB Recommendations 01/2020 require the parties to assess local laws and practices of the recipient country.
findings.recommendation: Reference a TIA / TRA in the DPA / SCC Annex and document the supplementary measures where needed.
findings.title: TIA / Transfer Risk Assessment reference missing

findings.description: Vaulytica did not find a governing-law clause.
findings.explanation: A governing-law clause picks the legal system that interprets the contract. Without it, courts apply conflict-of-laws rules, which may produce unexpected results.
findings.title: No governing-law clause detected

findings.description: No reference to written / electronic form was found.
findings.explanation: Art. 28(9) requires the contract to be in writing, including in electronic form.
findings.recommendation: State: 'This Agreement is in writing, including electronic form, in accordance with Article 28(9) GDPR.'
findings.title: Form-of-agreement clause missing

findings.description: No reference to ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability, and resilience was found.
findings.explanation: Art. 32(1)(b) requires measures to ensure ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services.
findings.recommendation: Add a clause referencing CIA-R (confidentiality, integrity, availability, resilience) of processing systems.
findings.title: Confidentiality / integrity / availability / resilience clause missing

findings.description: Breach-notice clause does not include 'without undue delay'.
findings.explanation: Art. 33(2) uses the 'without undue delay' standard; drafters should preserve this exact phrasing.
findings.recommendation: Add 'without undue delay' to the breach-notification clause.
findings.title: 'Without undue delay' language missing

findings.description: No clause was found regarding records of processing activities assistance.
findings.explanation: Art. 30 requires records of processing activities; processors typically assist controllers by providing relevant information.
findings.recommendation: Add a clause obligating the processor to provide information needed for the controller's Art. 30 RoPA.
findings.title: RoPA assistance clause missing

findings.description: No reference to an Art. 27 EU representative was found.
findings.explanation: Art. 27 requires non-EU controllers and processors to designate an EU representative (with exceptions).
findings.recommendation: If processor is non-EU, designate and name an Art. 27 EU representative in the DPA.
findings.title: Article 27 EU representative not referenced

findings.description: Could not detect the GDPR-style terms 'Personal Data Breach' or 'Data Subject.'
findings.explanation: Art. 4 defines the operative terms; modern DPAs use these capitalised defined terms throughout.
findings.recommendation: Define 'Personal Data Breach,' 'Data Subject,' 'Controller,' and 'Processor' cross-referencing Article 4 GDPR.
findings.title: GDPR-current terminology not present

findings.description: No governing-law clause was detected.
findings.explanation: GDPR does not specify governing law, but SCC clauses require a Member State choice; DPAs commonly mirror this.
findings.recommendation: Add a governing-law clause naming the applicable Member State (or UK) law.
findings.title: Governing-law clause missing

findings.description: No notice clause was detected.
findings.explanation: A notice clause anchors how breach notifications and other formal communications travel between the parties.
findings.recommendation: Add a notice clause naming the methods (email, certified mail), addresses, and timing requirements.
findings.title: Notice clause missing

findings.description: No survival clause was found extending obligations past termination.
findings.explanation: Where personal data is retained post-termination, the protective obligations should survive.
findings.recommendation: Add: 'Obligations applicable to retained Personal Data shall survive termination.'
findings.title: Survival clause missing

findings.description: No clause was found making subprocessor-change history available to the controller.
findings.explanation: Best-practice DPAs require the processor to maintain a record of subprocessor changes for the controller.
findings.recommendation: Add: 'Processor shall maintain a record of Sub-processors and changes thereto, available to Controller on request.'
findings.title: Records of subprocessor changes not referenced

findings.description: The contract does not allocate ownership of intellectual property.
findings.explanation: Without an IP-ownership clause, default copyright and patent rules apply: under 17 U.S.C. § 201, copyright vests in the author/employee unless work-for-hire or assignment applies.
findings.title: No IP-ownership clause detected

findings.description: Neither 'indemnify' nor 'hold harmless' appears in the document.
findings.explanation: Most commercial contracts allocate risk through an indemnification clause. The absence leaves the parties to default tort and contract law for any third-party claims.
findings.title: No indemnification clause detected

findings.description: Vaulytica did not find a limitation-of-liability clause.
findings.explanation: Without a limitation-of-liability clause, exposure is bounded only by what the parties can prove in damages. Most commercial contracts cap liability.
findings.title: No limitation-of-liability clause detected

findings.description: Used but not defined: Acme Corp, Data Subject Rights.
findings.explanation: Title-Case phrases in a contract usually signal a defined term. When they aren't defined, readers must guess at the intended meaning. Either define them or use lowercase if the term is intended in its ordinary sense.
findings.recommendation: Add a definition for each genuinely defined term, or change the casing if the phrase is being used in its ordinary sense.
findings.title: Undefined Title-Case terms: 2

findings.description: The contract does not state a path to terminate for material breach.
findings.explanation: Without a for-cause termination path, parties must rely on common-law material-breach doctrines, which are jurisdiction-dependent.
findings.title: No termination-for-cause clause detected

findings.description: The contract does not state what happens upon termination.
findings.explanation: An effect-of-termination clause spells out the rights and obligations that survive, the return or destruction of materials, and the wind-down rules.
findings.title: No effect-of-termination clause detected

findings.description: No fallback clause was found in case the adequacy decision is invalidated.
findings.explanation: Adequacy decisions can be invalidated (Schrems I, Schrems II). A fallback to SCCs / IDTA prevents transfer disruption.
findings.recommendation: Add: 'In the event the relied-upon adequacy decision is invalidated, the parties shall promptly implement the EU SCCs / UK Addendum / UK IDTA as a fallback.'
findings.title: Adequacy fallback clause missing

findings.description: The document does not state where disputes must be brought.
findings.explanation: Without a venue clause, default venue rules apply (in federal court, 28 U.S.C. § 1391). A clear forum-selection clause avoids ambiguity.
findings.title: No venue / forum clause detected

findings.description: The data importer shall not engage further sub-processors without prior specific written authorization of the data expor | The data importer shall not obstruct data subjects from exercising their GDPR rights and shall direct such requests to t

excerpt.text: The data importer shall not engage further sub-processors without prior specific written authorization of the data exporter, which authorization is itself subject to the upstream controller's prior written consent.

findings.explanation: Negative covenants restrict what a party may do. Surfacing them collectively makes it easier to check they are intended and consistent with the overall deal.
findings.title: Negative covenants: 2
